<skill_content name="customize-opencode">
# Skill: customize-opencode

<!--
  Built-in skill. Name and description are registered in code at
  packages/opencode/src/skill/index.ts (see CUSTOMIZE_OPENCODE_SKILL_NAME
  and CUSTOMIZE_OPENCODE_SKILL_DESCRIPTION). The body below becomes the
  skill's content.
-->

# Customizing opencode

opencode validates its own config strictly and refuses to start when a field
is wrong. The shapes below cover the common surface area, but they are a
**summary, not the source of truth**.

## Full schema reference

The authoritative list of every config option — with field types, enums,
defaults, and descriptions — lives in the published JSON Schema:

**<https://opencode.ai/config.json>**

If a field is not documented in this skill, or you need to confirm an exact
shape before writing config, **fetch that URL and read the schema directly**
rather than guessing. opencode hard-fails on invalid config, so the cost of a
wrong shape is a broken startup.

Independently, every `opencode.json` should declare
`"$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json"` so the user's editor catches
mistakes as they type.

## Applying changes

Config is loaded once when opencode starts and is not hot-reloaded. After
saving changes to `opencode.json`, an agent file, a skill, a plugin, or any
other config-time file, **tell the user to quit and restart opencode** for
the changes to take effect. The running session will keep using the
already-loaded config until then.

## Where files live

| Scope                         | Path                                                                                                                      |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Project config                | `./opencode.json`, `./opencode.jsonc`, or `.opencode/opencode.json` (opencode walks up from the cwd to the worktree root) |
| Global config                 | `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json` (NOT `~/.opencode/`)                                                                   |
| Project agents                | `.opencode/agent/<name>.md` or `.opencode/agents/<name>.md`                                                               |
| Global agents                 | `~/.config/opencode/agent(s)/<name>.md`                                                                                   |
| Project skills                | `.opencode/skill(s)/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                                                      |
| Global skills                 | `~/.config/opencode/skill(s)/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                                             |
| External skills (auto-loaded) | `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, `~/.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                    |

Configs from each scope are deep-merged. Project overrides global. Unknown
top-level keys in `opencode.json` are rejected with `ConfigInvalidError`.

## opencode.json

Every field is optional.

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
  "username": "string",
  "model": "provider/model-id",
  "small_model": "provider/model-id",
  "default_agent": "agent-name",
  "shell": "/bin/zsh",
  "logLevel": "DEBUG" | "INFO" | "WARN" | "ERROR",
  "share": "manual" | "auto" | "disabled",
  "autoupdate": true | false | "notify",
  "snapshot": true,
  "instructions": ["AGENTS.md", "docs/style.md"],

  "skills": {
    "paths": [".opencode/skills", "/abs/path/to/skills"],
    "urls": ["https://example.com/.well-known/skills/"]
  },

  "agent": {
    "my-agent": {
      "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "mode": "subagent",
      "description": "...",
      "permission": { "edit": "deny" }
    }
  },

  "command": {
    "deploy": { "description": "...", "prompt": "..." }
  },

  "provider": {
    "anthropic": { "options": { "apiKey": "..." } }
  },
  "disabled_providers": ["openai"],
  "enabled_providers": ["anthropic"],

  "mcp": {
    "playwright": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "@playwright/mcp"],
      "enabled": true,
      "env": {}
    },
    "remote-thing": {
      "type": "remote",
      "url": "https://...",
      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ..." }
    }
  },

  "plugin": [
    "opencode-gemini-auth",
    "opencode-foo@1.2.3",
    "./local-plugin.ts",
    ["opencode-bar", { "option": "value" }]
  ],

  "permission": {
    "edit": "deny",
    "bash": { "git *": "allow", "*": "ask" }
  },

  "formatter": false,
  "lsp": false,

  "experimental": {
    "primary_tools": ["edit"],
    "mcp_timeout": 30000
  },

  "tool_output": { "max_lines": 200, "max_bytes": 8192 },

  "compaction": { "auto": true, "tail_turns": 15 }
}
```

Shape notes worth being explicit about:

- `model` always carries a provider prefix: `"anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"`.
- `skills` is an object with `paths` and/or `urls`, not an array.
- `agent` is an object keyed by agent name, not an array.
- `plugin` is an array of strings or `[name, options]` tuples, not an object.
- `mcp[name].command` is an array of strings, never a single string. `type` is required.
- `permission` is either a string action or an object keyed by tool name.

## Skills

opencode's skill loader scans for `**/SKILL.md` inside skill directories. The
file is named `SKILL.md` exactly, and lives in its own folder named after the
skill:

```
.opencode/skills/my-skill/SKILL.md
```

Frontmatter:

```markdown
---
name: my-skill
description: One sentence covering what this skill does AND when to trigger it. Front-load the literal keywords or filenames the user is likely to say.
---

# My Skill

(skill body in markdown: instructions, examples, references)
```

- `name` is required, lowercase hyphen-separated, up to 64 chars, and matches the folder name.
- `description` is effectively required: skills without one are filtered out and never surfaced to the model. Cover both _what_ the skill does and _when_ to use it. Write in third person ("Use when...", not "I help with..."). Front-load concrete trigger keywords and filenames; gate with "Use ONLY when..." if the skill should stay quiet on adjacent topics.
- Optional: `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata` (string-string map).

Register skills from non-default locations via `skills.paths` (scanned
recursively for `**/SKILL.md`) and `skills.urls` (each URL serves a list of
skills).

## Agents

Two ways to define an agent. Use the file form for anything non-trivial.

### Inline (in `opencode.json`)

```json
{
  "agent": {
    "my-reviewer": {
      "description": "Reviews PRs for style violations.",
      "mode": "subagent",
      "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "permission": { "edit": "deny", "bash": "ask" },
      "prompt": "You are a strict PR reviewer..."
    }
  }
}
```

### File

```
.opencode/agent/my-reviewer.md      OR     .opencode/agents/my-reviewer.md
```

```markdown
---
description: Reviews PRs for style violations.
mode: subagent
model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6
permission:
  edit: deny
  bash: ask
---

You are a strict PR reviewer. Focus on...
```

The file body becomes the agent's `prompt`. Do not also put `prompt:` in the
frontmatter.

`mode` is one of `"primary"`, `"subagent"`, `"all"`.

Allowed top-level frontmatter fields: `name, model, variant, description, mode,
hidden, color, steps, options, permission, disable, temperature, top_p`. Any
unknown field is silently routed into `options`.

To disable a built-in agent: `agent: { build: { disable: true } }`, or in a
file, `disable: true` in frontmatter.

`default_agent` must point to a non-hidden, primary-mode agent.

### Built-in agents

opencode ships with `build`, `plan`, `general`, `explore`, plus optionally
`scout` (gated on `OPENCODE_EXPERIMENTAL_SCOUT`). Hidden internal agents:
`compaction`, `title`, `summary`. To override a built-in's fields, define the
same key in `agent: { <name>: { ... } }`.

## Plugins

`plugin:` is an array. Each entry is one of:

```json
"plugin": [
  "opencode-gemini-auth",            // npm spec, latest
  "opencode-foo@1.2.3",              // npm spec, pinned
  "./local-plugin.ts",               // file path, relative to the declaring config
  "file:///abs/path/plugin.js",      // file URL
  ["opencode-bar", { "key": "val" }] // tuple form with options
]
```

Auto-discovered plugins (no config entry needed): any `*.ts` or `*.js` file in
`.opencode/plugin/` or `.opencode/plugins/`.

A plugin module exports `default` (or any named export) of type
`Plugin = (input: PluginInput, options?) => Promise<Hooks>`. The export is a
function, not a plain object literal, and the function returns an object
(return `{}` if there is nothing to register).

```ts
import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"

export default (async ({ client, project, directory, $ }) => {
  return {
    config: (cfg) => {
      // cfg is the live merged config; mutate fields here.
    },
    "tool.execute.before": async (input, output) => {
      // mutate output.args before the tool runs
    },
  }
}) satisfies Plugin
```

Hook surface (mutate `output` in place; return `void`):

- `event(input)`: every bus event
- `config(cfg)`: once on init with the merged config
- `chat.message`, `chat.params`, `chat.headers`
- `tool.execute.before`, `tool.execute.after`
- `tool.definition`
- `command.execute.before`
- `shell.env`
- `permission.ask`
- `experimental.chat.messages.transform`, `experimental.chat.system.transform`,
  `experimental.session.compacting`, `experimental.compaction.autocontinue`,
  `experimental.text.complete`

Special object-shaped (not callbacks): `tool: { my_tool: { ... } }`,
`auth: { ... }`, `provider: { ... }`.

## MCP servers

`mcp:` is an object keyed by server name. Each server is discriminated by
`type`:

```json
{
  "mcp": {
    "playwright": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "@playwright/mcp"],
      "enabled": true,
      "env": { "BROWSER": "chromium" }
    },
    "github": {
      "type": "remote",
      "url": "https://...",
      "enabled": true,
      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ${GITHUB_TOKEN}" }
    },
    "old-server": { "enabled": false }
  }
}
```

`command` is an array of strings. `type` is required. Use `enabled: false` to
disable a server inherited from a parent config.

## Permissions

```json
"permission": {
  "edit": "deny",
  "bash": { "git *": "allow", "rm *": "deny", "*": "ask" },
  "external_directory": { "~/secrets/**": "deny", "*": "allow" }
}
```

Actions: `"allow"`, `"ask"`, `"deny"`.

Per-tool value forms: `"allow"` shorthand (treated as `{"*": "allow"}`), or an
object `{ pattern: action }`. Within an object, **insertion order matters**.
opencode evaluates the LAST matching rule, so put broad rules first and narrow
rules last.

`permission: "allow"` (a string at the top level) is shorthand for "allow
everything" and is rarely what the user wants.

Known permission keys: `read, edit, glob, grep, list, bash, task,
external_directory, todowrite, question, webfetch, websearch, codesearch,
repo_clone, repo_overview, lsp, doom_loop, skill`. Some of these (`todowrite,
question, webfetch, websearch, codesearch, doom_loop`) only accept a flat
action, not a per-pattern object.

`external_directory` patterns are filesystem paths (use `~/`, absolute paths,
or globs like `~/projects/**`).

Per-agent `permission:` overrides top-level `permission:`. Plan Mode lives on
the `plan` agent's permission ruleset (`edit: deny *`).

## Escape hatches

When a user's config is broken and opencode won't start, these env vars help:

- `OPENCODE_DISABLE_PROJECT_CONFIG=1`: skip the project's local `opencode.json`
  and start from globals only. Run from the project directory, opencode loads,
  the user edits the broken file, then they restart without the flag.
- `OPENCODE_CONFIG=/path/to/file.json`: load an additional explicit config.
- `OPENCODE_CONFIG_CONTENT='{"$schema":"https://opencode.ai/config.json"}'`:
  inject inline JSON as a final local-scope merge.
- `OPENCODE_DISABLE_DEFAULT_PLUGINS=1`: skip default plugins.
- `OPENCODE_PURE=1`: skip external plugins entirely.
- `OPENCODE_DISABLE_EXTERNAL_SKILLS=1`,
  `OPENCODE_DISABLE_CLAUDE_CODE_SKILLS=1`: skip the external skill scans under
  `~/.claude/` and `~/.agents/`.

## When proposing edits

- Validate against the schema before writing. If you are unsure of a field's
  exact shape, or the field is not covered in this skill, fetch
  `https://opencode.ai/config.json` and read the schema rather than guessing.
- Preserve `$schema` and any existing fields the user did not ask to change.
- For agent, skill, and plugin definitions, prefer creating new files in the
  correct location over inlining everything in `opencode.json`.
- If the user's existing config is malformed, point them at the env-var escape
  hatches above so they can edit from inside opencode without breaking their
  session.
- After saving any config change, remind the user to quit and restart opencode
  — running sessions keep using the already-loaded config.

parts.text:
**Investigating TUI configuration**

I need to inspect the relevant files. My initial glob search found .config/opencode/tui.json, but it wasn’t in the first glob. It seems the pattern didn’t capture tui. I should read the opencode JSON, the tui JSON, any documentation, and tmux. Also, checking .git status might show the deleted transparent theme. I wonder if fetching the opencode schema would help understand tui.json better, but maybe it’s not necessary? I need to investigate further.

The repo already has `tui.json` set to `theme: "system"` and tmux has explicit `copy-pipe-and-cancel` bindings, so I’m narrowing this to whether the deleted theme removed required transparent palette entries and whether the copy binding is being shadowed or failing at runtime.

30: # When a session is destroyed, switch to the most recently active session
31: # instead of detaching the client (which would close the terminal).
32: # Tested with tmux 3.5a.
33: set -g detach-on-destroy off
34: 
35: # Proactively ensure "main" session exists at server start.
36: # Combined with the session-closed hook below, this guarantees a fallback
37: # target is always available for detach-on-destroy.
38: # exit-empty is set to off so the server survives the brief window between
39: # sole-session destruction and hook recreation.
40: set -g exit-empty off
41: run-shell 'tmux has-session -t main 2>/dev/null || tmux new-session -d -s main'
42: 
43: # Reactively recreate "main" if destroyed while the server is still running.
44: set-hook -g session-closed 'if-shell "! tmux has-session -t main 2>/dev/null" "new-session -d -s main"'
45: 
46: # Activity monitoring - custom completion detection

53:

58: # Agent activity monitoring:

315: # Access with: Ctrl-s + T
316: bind T display-popup -E -h 80% -w 80% "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/tmux-session-trash.sh restore"
317: 
318: # Subscription Dashboard - Claude + OpenAI account usage overview
319: # Access with: Ctrl-s + M-d
320: bind M-d display-popup -E -h 85% -w 92% "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/subscription-dashboard.sh --compact"
321: 
322: # Which-key configuration (disabled - conflicts with Space binding)
323: # set -g @which-key-popup 1
324: 
325: # =============================================================================
326: # STATUS BAR CONFIGURATION - Three Section Layout
327: # =============================================================================
328: # ① Session (left) | ② Plugins (center) | ③ Windows (right/center)
329: # Powered by tmux-powerkit with rounded, pill-shaped segments
330: # =============================================================================
331: 
332: # Status bar layout configuration
333: set -g status-justify centre              # Center window list
334: set -g status-left-length 50              # Space for session indicator
335: set -g status-right-length 150            # Space for plugin pills
336: 
337: # Background styling (powerkit themes will override with their colors)
338: set -g status-style "bg=#1e1e2e,fg=#cdd6f4"
339: 
340: # Ensure pane backgrounds are not forced to a solid color
341: # (lets terminal/wezterm transparency show through and enables nvim winblend)
342: set -g window-style 'bg=default'
343: set -g window-active-style 'bg=default'
344: set -g popup-style 'bg=default,fg=default'
345: 
346: # Window status styling (powerkit may override, but provides fallback)
347: # Active window - highlighted with rounded background
348: setw -g window-status-current-style "fg=#1e1e2e,bg=#89b4fa,bold"
349: # Inactive windows - subdued appearance
350: setw -g window-status-style "fg=#6c7086,bg=#313244"
351: 
352: # Pane borders - subtle with accent on active
353: set -g pane-border-style "fg=#313244"
354: set -g pane-active-border-style "fg=#cba6f7"
355: 
356: # Message style
357: set -g message-style "bg=#313244,fg=#cdd6f4"
358: set -g message-command-style "bg=#313244,fg=#cdd6f4"
359: 
360: # Copy mode colors
361: set -g mode-style "bg=#45475a,fg=#cdd6f4"
362: 
363: # # Update status bar every 30 seconds for CPU/RAM (OPTIMIZED: reduced from 10s)
364: # # This reduces refresh overhead by 3x while still providing timely updates
365: # set -g status-interval 30
366: 
367: # Auto-install TPM if not present
368: if "test ! -d ~/.tmux/plugins/tpm" \
369:    "run 'git clone https://github.com/tmux-plugins/tpm ~/.tmux/plugins/tpm && ~/.tmux/plugins/tpm/bin/install_plugins'"
370: 
371: # Automatically split new windows and run claude (DISABLED)
372: # This creates a vertical split with claude in the right pane for every new window
373: # set-hook -g after-new-window 'split-window -h -p 40 "claude"'
374: 
375: # Manual keybinding to open Claude Code when needed
376: # Prefix + C opens Claude in a vertical split (40% width on the right)
377: bind C split-window -h -p 40 "claude --dangerously-skip-permissions"
378: 
379: # Quick launch: Ctrl-s a opens Claude with smart adaptive split
380: bind a if-shell -F "#{e|<:#{e|*:8,#{pane_width}},#{e|*:20,#{pane_height}}}" \
381:   "splitw -v -c '#{pane_current_path}' 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --effort max --remote-control' ; display '#[fg=#a6e3a1]↔ Split Horizontal (Claude)#[default]'" \
382:   "splitw -h -c '#{pane_current_path}' 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --effort max --remote-control' ; display '#[fg=#89dceb]↕ Split Vertical (Claude)#[default]'"
383: 
384: # Quick launch: Ctrl-s O opens OpenCode in its allow-permissions setup
385: bind O if-shell -F "#{e|<:#{e|*:8,#{pane_width}},#{e|*:20,#{pane_height}}}" \
386:   "splitw -v -c '#{pane_current_path}' '$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/opencode/tmux-open.sh' ; display '#[fg=#a6e3a1]↔ Split Horizontal (OpenCode)#[default]'" \
387:   "splitw -h -c '#{pane_current_path}' '$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/opencode/tmux-open.sh' ; display '#[fg=#89dceb]↕ Split Vertical (OpenCode)#[default]'"
388: 
389: # Quick launch: Ctrl-s e opens Neovim in current directory
390: # C-c first to normalize Fish vi-mode state (lands us in insert mode at empty
391: # prompt) so the typed `nvim .` isn't eaten as vi-mode normal commands.
392: bind e send-keys C-c "nvim ." Enter
393: 
394: # Quick launch: Ctrl-s E opens per-directory gwtt-prompt.local.md with smart adaptive split
395: bind E if-shell -F "#{e|<:#{e|*:8,#{pane_width}},#{e|*:20,#{pane_height}}}" \
396:   "splitw -v -c '#{pane_current_path}' '$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/gwtt-prompt-edit.sh' ; display '#[fg=#a6e3a1]↔ Split Horizontal (gwtt-prompt)#[default]'" \
397:   "splitw -h -c '#{pane_current_path}' '$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/gwtt-prompt-edit.sh' ; display '#[fg=#89dceb]↕ Split Vertical (gwtt-prompt)#[default]'"
398: 
399: # URL handler binding (smart: tries visible pane first, falls back to full scrollback)
400: bind-key o run-shell -b "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/tmux-url-handler.sh"
401: 
402: # Keep default tmux bindings for navigation
403: # Prefix + O: select previous pane
404: # Prefix + p: previous window (default tmux behavior)
405: 
406: # Initialize TMUX plugin manager (keep this line at the very bottom of tmux.conf)
407: run '~/.tmux/plugins/tpm/tpm'
408: 
409: # Force tmux-open bindings after all plugins load
410: run-shell '~/.tmux/plugins/tmux-open/open.tmux'
411: 
412: # Restore prefix+x to kill-pane (extrakto plugin overrides this even with @extrakto_key changed)
413: # Enhanced: capture window info before killing for worktree auto-cleanup.
414: # Run the wrapper in tmux background so pane close stays responsive.
415: bind-key x run-shell -b '~/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/tmux-kill-pane-cleanup.sh "#{session_name}" "#{window_name}" "#{window_panes}"'
416: 
417: # Kill window: prefix + X (Shift-x) - closes entire window with worktree cleanup
418: bind-key X confirm-before -p 'Kill window "#{window_name}"? (y/n)' \
419:   "run-shell -b '~/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/tmux-worktree-cleanup.sh \"#{session_name}\" \"#{window_name}\"'; kill-window"
420: 
421: # Force status bar position and layout (override plugin defaults)
422: set -g status-position bottom
423: set -g status-justify centre
424: 
425: # Final overrides to keep transparent panes/popups after plugins load
426: set -g window-style 'bg=default'
427: set -g window-active-style 'bg=default'
428: set -g popup-style 'bg=default,fg=default'
429: 
430: # Override powerkit's default 5s status-interval (must be after TPM)
431: set -g status-interval 15
432: 
433: # Fix nested fish: reset default-command to empty after TPM loads.
434: # tmux-sensible (loaded by TPM) overrides empty default-command with
435: # "reattach-to-user-namespace -l $SHELL". By setting it to fish BEFORE TPM,
436: # sensible sees non-empty value and leaves it alone. Now we reset to empty
437: # so tmux launches default-shell directly (no "fish -c fish" nesting).
438: set -g default-command ''
439: 
440: # Inject @wname_style into status bar window tabs (must be after TPM/powerkit)
441: # Makes agent state colors visible in the status bar, not just choose-tree
442: run-shell '~/dotfiles/scripts/tmux/inject-wname-style.sh'
443: 
444: # Colored choose-tree session/window picker (must be after TPM)
445: # Tree labels (session/window names, markers) use pane fg — set via select-pane -P
446: # pane-mode-changed hook resets pane style when exiting choose-tree
447: # Agent colors set by watcher via @wname_style: running=green, idle=yellow, stuck=red
448: set-hook -g pane-mode-changed[100] 'if -F "#{pane_in_mode}" "" "select-pane -P default"'
449: bind s select-pane -P 'fg=#9ece6a' \; choose-tree -Zw -F '#{?pane_format,#[fg=#7dcfff]#{pane_current_command} #[fg=#565f89](#{pane_current_path}),#{?window_format,#{@wname_style}#{window_name}#{?window_active, *,},#[fg=#565f89]#{session_windows} windows#{?session_attached, #[fg=#e0af68](attached),}}}'
450: bind w select-pane -P 'fg=#9ece6a' \; choose-tree -Zw -F '#{?pane_format,#[fg=#7dcfff]#{pane_current_command} #[fg=#565f89](#{pane_current_path}),#{?window_format,#{@wname_style}#{window_name}#{?window_active, *,},#[fg=#565f89]#{session_windows} windows#{?session_attached, #[fg=#e0af68](attached),}}}'
451: 
452: # Context-aware r key: recall when in Claude, config reload otherwise
453: # Must be after TPM to override tmux-powerkit's reload binding
454: bind r if-shell "ps -o args= -t #{pane_tty} 2>/dev/null | grep -q 'claude'" \
455:     "confirm-before -p 'Kill Claude pane and run recall? (y/n)' 'respawn-pane -k \"/opt/homebrew/bin/recall\"'" \
456:     "source-file ~/.tmux.conf \\; display '#[fg=#a6e3a1]󰑓 Config Reloaded#[default]'"

(End of file - total 456 lines)
</content>

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Launch OpenCode inside tmux with alternate-screen disabled so tmux copy-mode
# retains scrollback. Restores the prior setting when OpenCode exits.
set -euo pipefail

# Determine current window id so we can scope setw calls via tmux target
WINDOW=${TMUX_PANE:-}

restore_alternate_screen() {
	if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
		tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
	else
		tmux setw -w alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
	fi
}

trap restore_alternate_screen EXIT

if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
	tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen off >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

state.output:
<path>/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-nvim.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # opencode.nvim vs tmux Side Pane
2: 
3: OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane. The terminal binary is `ocv` from [`leohenon/opencode-vim`](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim), exposed through the repo-managed `scripts/bin/opencode` shim so existing launchers keep working while the TUI gains Vim motions, copy mode, and clipboard-register support. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin is the complementary Neovim integration layer: it keeps the same backend assistant but embeds editor context directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
4: 
5: ## Why the plugin is a material upgrade
6: 
7: - **Zero-copy context** – prompts can reference placeholders such as `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, or `@visible`. The plugin resolves those against your current buffer/selection before sending them to OpenCode, so you stop yanking snippets into the side pane.
8: - **Prompt + operator ergonomics** – `require("opencode").ask`, `select`, and the operator wrapper let you reuse visual selections, ranges, and dot-repeat. Side panes are strictly linear terminals with no operator integration.
9: - **Editor-driven review loop** – when OpenCode proposes edits, the plugin opens a tab with `:diffpatch`, lets you accept/reject per hunk (`dp`, `do`, `]c`, `[c`), and reloads buffers on approval. The side pane requires manual patch application or copy/paste.
10: - **Permission + event awareness** – server-sent events surface inside Neovim via the `OpencodeEvent` autocmd, so you can hook custom automation (notifications, statusline, etc.). The pane view only shows whatever the TUI prints.
11: - **Experimental LSP bridge** – enabling `vim.g.opencode_opts.lsp.enabled` turns hover/code-action requests into OpenCode prompts. There is no equivalent API from the tmux pane.
12: - **Session UX** – the plugin auto-starts/stops OpenCode servers, exposes commands (`session.select`, `session.interrupt`, etc.) through pickers, and includes optional Snacks integrations. Launching a pane simply spawns `opencode` and leaves lifecycle management to you.
13: 
14: ## What stays the same
15: 
16: - The backend assistant is still OpenCode-compatible (`opencode` resolves to `ocv`), so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
17: - You can connect the plugin to any running OpenCode instance via the `server.port` option, which means the tmux binding can keep a long-lived side pane if you want a dedicated transcript while Neovim handles buffer-aware asks.
18: - Permissions and hooks still flow through the existing `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts` stack—`opencode.nvim` just surfaces them in-editor.
19: 
20: ## Comparison snapshot
21: 
22: | Capability | tmux side pane | `opencode.nvim` |
23: |------------|----------------|-----------------|
24: | Context capture | Manual copy/paste | Automatic via placeholders (`@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, etc.) |
25: | Prompt ergonomics | TUI input only | Lua API + Snacks picker/input + operator support + dot-repeat |
26: | Edit review | Copy/paste or apply patch manually | Built-in diff tab with accept/reject bindings (`da`, `dr`, `dp`, `do`) |
27: | Permission handling | Raw JSON/log output | Inline prompts after idle + autocmd hooks |
28: | Session control | `opencode` CLI shortcuts | `require("opencode").command("session.*")` + configurable keymaps |
29: | LSP integration | None | Optional hover/code-action bridge |
30: | Statusline signal | Pane title only | `require("opencode").statusline` component |
31: 
32: ## Adoption guidance
33: 
34: 1. Install the plugin via LazyVim (see upstream README snippet) and set `vim.g.opencode_opts.server.port` to match the port our scripts already use (or let it spawn its own terminal).
35: 2. Keep `vim.o.autoread = true` so edits from OpenCode reload correctly.
36: 3. Reuse the recommended keymaps (`<C-a>` ask, `<C-x>` select, `go` operator) or map them under `<leader>o` to avoid conflicts with tmux bindings.
37: 4. Run `:checkhealth opencode` after wiring it up; the check validates that OpenCode is discoverable and events are flowing.
38: 5. Continue to use the tmux pane when you need a dedicated transcript view or when Neovim is not open—both entry points can coexist because they talk to the same OpenCode backend.
39: 
40: ## opencode-vim and opencode.nvim together
41: 
42: - `ocv` replaces the terminal TUI and provides full Vim-mode ergonomics when OpenCode runs in tmux or a standalone shell.
43: - `scripts/bin/opencode` preserves the command name expected by `gwt-ticket`, tmux launchers, auth rotation, cross-provider hooks, and `opencode.nvim`.
44: - `opencode.nvim` keeps using `opencode --port`; the shim routes that to `ocv --port`, so editor-native prompts inherit the same binary and plugin stack.
45: - `.config/opencode/tui.json` stays close to the documented OpenCode schema: it uses the built-in `system` theme for terminal transparency, disables mouse capture, and only overrides a few navigation shortcuts.
46: - Use `Ctrl-x y` to copy the selected/current message from OpenCode. For arbitrary transcript text, use tmux copy mode: `Ctrl-s [` or `Ctrl-s v`, select with `v`, then yank with `y` or `Enter`.
47: 
48: By moving repetitive prompt + context work into Neovim we remove most of the friction highlighted in this ticket while leaving the tmux bindings available for workflows outside the editor.
49: 
50: ## OpenCode-only Neovim workflow
51: 
52: `opencode.nvim` is now the primary in-editor AI layer. It owns buffer-aware prompts, visual selections, operator workflows, and the diff review loop. The tmux side pane remains useful for long transcripts and non-editor sessions; no secondary editor-assistant plugin is part of the active workflow.
53: 
54: ### Reference Lua snippet
55: 
56: ```lua
57: -- lazy.nvim spec excerpt (~/neovim/lua/plugins/opencode.lua)
58: return {
59:   "nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim",
60:   dependencies = { "folke/snacks.nvim" },
61:   config = function()
62:     vim.g.opencode_opts = {
63:       server = {
64:         port = tonumber(vim.env.OPENCODE_PORT or 3333),
65:       },
66:       prompts = {
67:         diagnostics = { name = "diagnostics", prompt = "Explain @diagnostics" },
68:         refactor = { name = "refactor", prompt = "Refactor @this for readability" },
69:       },
70:     }
71:     vim.keymap.set({ "n", "x" }, "<C-a>", function()
72:       require("opencode").ask("@this: ", { submit = true })
73:     end, { desc = "Ask OpenCode" })
74:     vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("User", {
75:       pattern = "OpencodeEvent:*",
76:       callback = function(args)
77:         require("opencode.status").update(args.data.event)
78:       end,
79:     })
80:   end,
81: }
82: ```
83: 
84: With this setup you can define explicit workflows:
85: 
86: - **Diagnostics review** – `:lua require("opencode").ask("Explain @diagnostics and propose the smallest fix", { submit = true })`.
87: - **Refactor selection** – visual select code, hit `go` to enqueue via the operator, then accept/reject hunks in the diff tab.
88: - **Diff review** – `:lua require("opencode").ask("Review @diff for correctness, regressions, and missing tests", { submit = true })`.
89: 
90: ## SSE logging + Entire checkpoints
91: 
92: - `.config/opencode/plugin/sse-recorder.ts` listens to every OpenCode SSE event, writes a JSONL audit log under `.entire/opencode/sse/events.jsonl`, and mirrors summary payloads into `entire hooks opencode sse-event` so checkpoints capture turn-by-turn metadata.
93: - Whenever an event contains a diff/patch payload, the plugin stores a timestamped snapshot inside `.entire/opencode/sse/diffs/` and emits a secondary `sse-diff` hook with the file path.
94: - The harness `scripts/opencode/test-sse-recorder.ts` exercises the plugin in isolation to guarantee log + diff files are produced.
95: - Use `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh --cat` (or `--meta`) to inspect the newest AI patch. Diffview can read those patch files through its existing tmux/Zsh discovery hooks, so you can replay AI-generated edits even after closing the OpenCode TUI.
96: 
97: ## Diffview integration pointers
98: 
99: 1. Keep `.config/fish/conf.d/diffview-follow.fish` enabled so every `cd` notifies Neovim’s Diffview window about repo changes.
100: 2. When the SSE recorder drops a new patch, run `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh` to get the file path, then inside Neovim run `:DiffviewFileHistory <patch>` or open it in a scratch buffer for review.
101: 3. Because the recorder stores metadata (`*.patch.json`), you can show the originating session/message in statuslines or in Entire checkpoints.
102: 
103: ## Automated validation
104: 
105: `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` now covers the new surfaces:
106: 
107: - `scripts/opencode/test-sse-recorder.ts` (Bun harness) ensures the recorder plugin logs events/diffs.
108: - `scripts/opencode/test-nvim-health.sh` runs `nvim --headless` → `luafile` (module probes for `opencode` and `wrapped`) → `:checkhealth opencode` so CI flags missing OpenCode editor integration immediately. Set `OPENCODE_NVIM_APPNAME` if you use a non-default Neovim profile.
109: - `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh` gives tooling + tests a stable entry point for the most recent diff snapshot.
110: 
111: Together these cover the “hybrid UI + SSE logging + Diffview replay” workflow discussed in the ticket.
112: 
113: ## Wrapped.nvim dashboard
114: 
115: - `aikhe/wrapped.nvim` is installed (Lazy spec in `~/neovim/lua/plugins/wrapped.lua`) with `nvzone/volt` so you can launch a dashboard of repo activity via `:WrappedNvim` or the shortcut `<leader>aw`.
116: - The dashboard pulls git stats from `vim.fn.stdpath("config")` (override with `NVIM_WRAPPED_PATH`) and renders commit heatmaps, plugin growth charts, and large-file summaries. Size/border are tuned for tmux panes via rounded border + 75% of current editor dimensions.
117: - Use `<` / `>` to cycle years, `r` to refresh after new commits, and `q` to close. Because data collection is async, a loading screen appears until git/file/plugin tasks finish.
118: - Pairing this dashboard with the SSE recorder means Entire checkpoints capture both AI session history and evolving Neovim configuration history (for example, referencing the most active month when reviewing opencode usage).

(End of file - total 118 lines)
</content>

OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane. The terminal binary is `ocv` from [`leohenon/opencode-vim`](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim), exposed through the repo-managed `scripts/bin/opencode` shim so existing launchers keep working while the TUI gains Vim motions, copy mode, and clipboard-register support. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin is the complementary Neovim integration layer: it keeps the same backend assistant but embeds editor context directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.

## Why the plugin is a material upgrade

- **Zero-copy context** – prompts can reference placeholders such as `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, or `@visible`. The plugin resolves those against your current buffer/selection before sending them to OpenCode, so you stop yanking snippets into the side pane.
- **Prompt + operator ergonomics** – `require("opencode").ask`, `select`, and the operator wrapper let you reuse visual selections, ranges, and dot-repeat. Side panes are strictly linear terminals with no operator integration.
- **Editor-driven review loop** – when OpenCode proposes edits, the plugin opens a tab with `:diffpatch`, lets you accept/reject per hunk (`dp`, `do`, `]c`, `[c`), and reloads buffers on approval. The side pane requires manual patch application or copy/paste.
- **Permission + event awareness** – server-sent events surface inside Neovim via the `OpencodeEvent` autocmd, so you can hook custom automation (notifications, statusline, etc.). The pane view only shows whatever the TUI prints.
- **Experimental LSP bridge** – enabling `vim.g.opencode_opts.lsp.enabled` turns hover/code-action requests into OpenCode prompts. There is no equivalent API from the tmux pane.
- **Session UX** – the plugin auto-starts/stops OpenCode servers, exposes commands (`session.select`, `session.interrupt`, etc.) through pickers, and includes optional Snacks integrations. Launching a pane simply spawns `opencode` and leaves lifecycle management to you.

## What stays the same

- The backend assistant is still OpenCode-compatible (`opencode` resolves to `ocv`), so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
- You can connect the plugin to any running OpenCode instance via the `server.port` option, which means the tmux binding can keep a long-lived side pane if you want a dedicated transcript while Neovim handles buffer-aware asks.
- Permissions and hooks still flow through the existing `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts` stack—`opencode.nvim` just surfaces them in-editor.

## Comparison snapshot

<content>
1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # tmux-copy-cleanup.sh - Clean up copied text from tmux copy-mode
3: # Removes spurious line breaks caused by terminal soft-wrapping
4: # Used as a copy-pipe target in tmux for "clean copy" operations
5: #
6: # Usage in tmux:
7: #   bind-key -T copy-mode-vi Y send-keys -X copy-pipe 'tmux-copy-cleanup.sh'
8: #
9: # Mode: $1 (optional)
10: #   "join"       - Join all lines (default, best for commands)
11: #   "smart"      - Preserve paragraph breaks, join wrapped lines
12: #   "passthrough" - Just pass to clipboard (same as plain pbcopy)
13: 
14: set -euo pipefail
15: 
16: MODE="${1:-join}"
17: 
18: # Read stdin (text from tmux copy-pipe)
19: INPUT=$(cat)
20: 
21: case "$MODE" in
22: join)
23:     # Join all lines, collapse whitespace - ideal for commands
24:     printf '%s' "$INPUT" | tr '\n' ' ' | sed 's/  */ /g; s/^ *//; s/ *$//'
25:     ;;
26: smart)
27:     # Preserve paragraph breaks (double newlines), join single newlines
28:     printf '%s' "$INPUT" | awk '
29:             BEGIN { para = "" }
30:             /^[[:space:]]*$/ {
31:                 if (para != "") { print para; print ""; para = "" }
32:                 next
33:             }
34:             {
35:                 sub(/[[:space:]]+$/, "")
36:                 if (para == "") { para = $0 }
37:                 else { para = para " " $0 }
38:             }
39:             END { if (para != "") print para }
40:         '
41:     ;;
42: passthrough)
43:     printf '%s' "$INPUT"
44:     ;;
45: esac | if [ "$(uname -s)" = "Darwin" ]; then
46:     pbcopy
47: else
48:     xclip -selection clipboard 2>/dev/null || xsel --clipboard --input 2>/dev/null || true
49: fi

(End of file - total 49 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

for ocv_bin in /opt/homebrew/bin/ocv /usr/local/bin/ocv "$HOME/.local/bin/ocv" "$HOME/.bun/bin/ocv"; do
	if [ -x "$ocv_bin" ]; then
		exec "$ocv_bin" "$@"
	fi
done

if ! command -v ocv >/dev/null 2>&1; then
	printf 'opencode shim: ocv is not installed. Run: brew install leohenon/tap/ocv\n' >&2
	exit 127
fi

exec ocv "$@"

Ultimately, using `ocv --help` in read-only mode seems like a good starting point, along with checking the version using Bash. I want to keep it straightforward!

# Themes

Select a built-in theme or define your own.

With OpenCode you can select from one of several built-in themes, use a theme that adapts to your terminal theme, or define your own custom theme.

By default, OpenCode uses our own `opencode` theme.

---

## [Terminal requirements](#terminal-requirements)

For themes to display correctly with their full color palette, your terminal must support **truecolor** (24-bit color). Most modern terminals support this by default, but you may need to enable it:

-   **Check support**: Run `echo $COLORTERM` - it should output `truecolor` or `24bit`
-   **Enable truecolor**: Set the environment variable `COLORTERM=truecolor` in your shell profile
-   **Terminal compatibility**: Ensure your terminal emulator supports 24-bit color (most modern terminals like iTerm2, Alacritty, Kitty, Windows Terminal, and recent versions of GNOME Terminal do)

Without truecolor support, themes may appear with reduced color accuracy or fall back to the nearest 256-color approximation.

---

## [Built-in themes](#built-in-themes)

OpenCode comes with several built-in themes.

Name

Description

`system`

Adapts to your terminal’s background color

`tokyonight`

Based on the [Tokyonight](https://github.com/folke/tokyonight.nvim) theme

`everforest`

Based on the [Everforest](https://github.com/sainnhe/everforest) theme

`ayu`

Based on the [Ayu](https://github.com/ayu-theme) dark theme

`catppuccin`

Based on the [Catppuccin](https://github.com/catppuccin) theme

`catppuccin-macchiato`

Based on the [Catppuccin](https://github.com/catppuccin) theme

`gruvbox`

Based on the [Gruvbox](https://github.com/morhetz/gruvbox) theme

`kanagawa`

Based on the [Kanagawa](https://github.com/rebelot/kanagawa.nvim) theme

`nord`

Based on the [Nord](https://github.com/nordtheme/nord) theme

`matrix`

Hacker-style green on black theme

`one-dark`

Based on the [Atom One](https://github.com/Th3Whit3Wolf/one-nvim) Dark theme

And more, we are constantly adding new themes.

---

## [System theme](#system-theme)

The `system` theme is designed to automatically adapt to your terminal’s color scheme. Unlike traditional themes that use fixed colors, the *system* theme:

-   **Generates gray scale**: Creates a custom gray scale based on your terminal’s background color, ensuring optimal contrast.
-   **Uses ANSI colors**: Leverages standard ANSI colors (0-15) for syntax highlighting and UI elements, which respect your terminal’s color palette.
-   **Preserves terminal defaults**: Uses `none` for text and background colors to maintain your terminal’s native appearance.

The system theme is for users who:

-   Want OpenCode to match their terminal’s appearance
-   Use custom terminal color schemes
-   Prefer a consistent look across all terminal applications

---

## [Using a theme](#using-a-theme)

You can select a theme by bringing up the theme select with the `/theme` command. Or you can specify it in `tui.json`.

tui.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/tui.json",  "theme": "tokyonight"}
```

---

## [Custom themes](#custom-themes)

OpenCode supports a flexible JSON-based theme system that allows users to create and customize themes easily.

---

### [Hierarchy](#hierarchy)

Themes are loaded from multiple directories in the following order where later directories override earlier ones:

1.  **Built-in themes** - These are embedded in the binary
2.  **User config directory** - Defined in `~/.config/opencode/themes/*.json` or `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/opencode/themes/*.json`
3.  **Project root directory** - Defined in the `<project-root>/.opencode/themes/*.json`
4.  **Current working directory** - Defined in `./.opencode/themes/*.json`

If multiple directories contain a theme with the same name, the theme from the directory with higher priority will be used.

---

### [Creating a theme](#creating-a-theme)

To create a custom theme, create a JSON file in one of the theme directories.

For user-wide themes:

Terminal window

```
mkdir -p ~/.config/opencode/themesvim ~/.config/opencode/themes/my-theme.json
```

And for project-specific themes.

Terminal window

```
mkdir -p .opencode/themesvim .opencode/themes/my-theme.json
```

---

### [JSON format](#json-format)

Themes use a flexible JSON format with support for:

-   **Hex colors**: `"#ffffff"`
-   **ANSI colors**: `3` (0-255)
-   **Color references**: `"primary"` or custom definitions
-   **Dark/light variants**: `{"dark": "#000", "light": "#fff"}`
-   **No color**: `"none"` - Uses the terminal’s default color or transparent

---

### [Color definitions](#color-definitions)

The `defs` section is optional and it allows you to define reusable colors that can be referenced in the theme.

---

### [Terminal defaults](#terminal-defaults)

The special value `"none"` can be used for any color to inherit the terminal’s default color. This is particularly useful for creating themes that blend seamlessly with your terminal’s color scheme:

-   `"text": "none"` - Uses terminal’s default foreground color
-   `"background": "none"` - Uses terminal’s default background color

---

### [Example](#example)

Here’s an example of a custom theme:

my-theme.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/theme.json",  "defs": {    "nord0": "#2E3440",    "nord1": "#3B4252",    "nord2": "#434C5E",    "nord3": "#4C566A",    "nord4": "#D8DEE9",    "nord5": "#E5E9F0", # TUI

Using the OpenCode terminal user interface.

OpenCode provides an interactive terminal interface or TUI for working on your projects with an LLM.

Running OpenCode starts the TUI for the current directory.

Terminal window

```
opencode
```

Or you can start it for a specific working directory.

Terminal window

```
opencode /path/to/project
```

Once you’re in the TUI, you can prompt it with a message.

```
Give me a quick summary of the codebase.
```

---

## [File references](#file-references)

You can reference files in your messages using `@`. This does a fuzzy file search in the current working directory.

Tip

You can also use `@` to reference files in your messages.

```
How is auth handled in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts?
```

The content of the file is added to the conversation automatically.

Configured references also appear in `@` autocomplete. Type `@alias` to add the reference root as context, or type `@alias/` to autocomplete files inside that reference.

```
Compare our setup with @docs/README.md
```

---

## [Bash commands](#bash-commands)

Start a message with `!` to run a shell command.

```
!ls -la
```

The output of the command is added to the conversation as a tool result.

---

## [Commands](#commands)

When using the OpenCode TUI, you can type `/` followed by a command name to quickly execute actions. For example:

```
/help
```

Most commands also have keyboard shortcuts using `ctrl+x` as the default leader key. [Learn more](/docs/keybinds).

Here are all available slash commands:

---

### [connect](#connect)

Add a provider to OpenCode. Allows you to select from available providers and add their API keys.

```
/connect
```

---

### [compact](#compact)

Compact the current session. *Alias*: `/summarize`

```
/compact
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x c`

---

### [details](#details)

Toggle tool execution details.

```
/details
```

---

### [editor](#editor)

Open external editor for composing messages. Uses the editor set in your `EDITOR` environment variable. [Learn more](#editor-setup).

```
/editor
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x e`

---

### [exit](#exit)

Exit OpenCode. *Aliases*: `/quit`, `/q`

```
/exit
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x q`

---

### [export](#export)

Export current conversation to Markdown and open in your default editor. Uses the editor set in your `EDITOR` environment variable. [Learn more](#editor-setup).

```
/export
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x x`

---

### [help](#help)

Show the help dialog.

```
/help
```

---

### [init](#init)

Guided setup for creating or updating `AGENTS.md`. [Learn more](/docs/rules).

```
/init
```

---

### [models](#models)

List available models.

```
/models
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x m`

---

### [new](#new)

Start a new session. *Alias*: `/clear`

```
/new
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x n`

---

### [redo](#redo)

Redo a previously undone message. Only available after using `/undo`.

Tip

Any file changes will also be restored.

Internally, this uses Git to manage the file changes. So your project **needs to be a Git repository**.

```
/redo
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x r`

---

### [sessions](#sessions)

List and switch between sessions. *Aliases*: `/resume`, `/continue`

```
/sessions
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x l`

---

### [share](#share)

Share current session. [Learn more](/docs/share).

```
/share
```

---

### [themes](#themes)

List available themes.

```
/themes
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x t`

---

### [thinking](#thinking)

Toggle the visibility of thinking/reasoning blocks in the conversation. When enabled, you can see the model’s reasoning process for models that support extended thinking.

Note

This command only controls whether thinking blocks are **displayed** - it does not enable or disable the model’s reasoning capabilities. To toggle actual reasoning capabilities, use `ctrl+t` to cycle through model variants.

```
/thinking
```

---

### [undo](#undo)

Undo last message in the conversation. Removes the most recent user message, all subsequent responses, and any file changes.

Tip

Any file changes made will also be reverted.

Internally, this uses Git to manage the file changes. So your project **needs to be a Git repository**.

```
/undo
```

**Keybind:** `ctrl+x u`

---

### [unshare](#unshare)

Unshare current session. [Learn more](/docs/share#un-sharing).

```
/unshare
```

---

## [Editor setup](#editor-setup)

Both the `/editor` and `/export` commands use the editor specified in your `EDITOR` environment variable.

-   [Linux/macOS](#tab-panel-4)
-   [Windows (CMD)](#tab-panel-5)
-   [Windows (PowerShell)](#tab-panel-6)

Terminal window

```
# Example for nano or vimexport EDITOR=nanoexport EDITOR=vim
# For GUI editors, VS Code, Cursor, VSCodium, Windsurf, Zed, etc.# include --waitexport EDITOR="code --wait"
```

To make it permanent, add this to your shell profile; `~/.bashrc`, `~/.zshrc`, etc.

Terminal window

```
set EDITOR=notepad
# For GUI editors, VS Code, Cursor, VSCodium, Windsurf, Zed, etc.# include --waitset EDITOR=code --wait
```

To make it permanent, use **System Properties** > **Environment Variables**.

Terminal window

```
$env:EDITOR = "notepad"
# For GUI editors, VS Code, Cursor, VSCodium, Windsurf, Zed, etc.# include --wait$env:EDITOR = "code --wait"
```

To make it permanent, add this to your PowerShell profile.

Popular editor options include:

-   `code` - Visual Studio Code
-   `cursor` - Cursor
-   `windsurf` - Windsurf
-   `nvim` - Neovim editor
-   `vim` - Vim editor
-   `nano` - Nano editor
-   `notepad` - Windows Notepad
-   `subl` - Sublime Text

Note

Some editors like VS Code need to be started with the `--wait` flag.

Some editors need command-line arguments to run in blocking mode. The `--wait` flag makes the editor process block until closed.

---

## [Configure](#configure)

You can customize TUI behavior through `tui.json` (or `tui.jsonc`).

tui.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/tui.json",  "theme": "opencode",  "leader_timeout": 2000,  "keybinds": {    "leader": "ctrl+x",    "command_list": "ctrl+p"  },  "scroll_speed": 3,  "scroll_acceleration": {    "enabled": false  },  "diff_style": "auto",  "mouse": true}
```

This is separate from `opencode.json`, which configures server/runtime behavior.

`keybinds` is merged with built-in defaults, so you only need to configure the shortcuts you want to change.

### [Options](#options)

-   `theme` - Sets your UI theme. [Learn more](/docs/themes).
-   `keybinds` - Customizes keyboard shortcuts. [Learn more](/docs/keybinds).
-   `leader_timeout` - Controls how long OpenCode waits after the leader key. Defaults to `2000`.
-   `scroll_acceleration.enabled` - Enable macOS-style scroll acceleration for smooth, natural scrolling. When enabled, scroll speed increases with rapid scrolling gestures and stays precise for slower movements. **This setting takes precedence over `scroll_speed` and overrides it when enabled.**
-   `scroll_speed` - Controls how fast the TUI scrolls when using scroll commands (minimum: `0.001`, supports decimal values). Defaults to `3`. **Note: This is ignored if `scroll_acceleration.enabled` is set to `true`.**
-   `diff_style` - Controls diff rendering. `"auto"` adapts to terminal width, `"stacked"` always shows a single-column layout.
-   `mouse` - Enable or disable mouse capture in the TUI (default: `true`). When disabled, the terminal’s native mouse selection/scrolling behavior is preserved.

Use `OPENCODE_TUI_CONFIG` to load a custom TUI config path.

---

## [Customization](#customization)

You can customize various aspects of the TUI view using the command palette (`ctrl+p`). These settings persist across restarts.

---

#### [Username display](#username-display)

Toggle whether your username appears in chat messages. Access this through:

-   Command palette: Search for “username” or “hide username”
-   The setting persists automatically and will be remembered across TUI sessions

parts.text:
**Considering transparency in themes**

The user mentioned a concern about the system not being fully transparent, likely due to the backgroundPanel or backgroundElement settings. Since the documentation states it uses “none” for text and background, I wonder if the panels are still shaded. They previously deleted the transparent theme but now find Opencode isn't fully transparent.

I think the best plan may be to either restore the minimal transparent theme or create a custom theme to ensure full transparency. It seems important to clarify their exact preference here.

**Evaluating theme options**

I’m considering naming the new theme either `system-transparent` or `tokyonight-transparent`. Since the previous “transparent theme” was deleted but Opencode isn't fully transparent now, my plan might involve reintroducing a minimal custom theme instead of the older, more complex one. However, the user might prefer not to have a separate theme. It could be helpful to check the git history for earlier deletions using commands like `git log --name-status`. I’ll look into that next!

**Planning theme edits**

I need to inspect the deleted theme and the previous TUI to plan my edits accurately. I'll use the command `git show 66a?` to review the deleted file before the cleanup. Specifically, I want to look at `66a2510a^:.config/opencode/themes/transparent.json`. I'll access it in read-only mode via bash. Comparing the current version to the previous one might be necessary, and I won't use `cat` for the output. Let's see what the theme file shows!

1: [![Build
2: Status](https://travis-ci.org/tmux-plugins/tmux-yank.svg?branch=master)](https://travis-ci.org/tmux-plugins/tmux-yank)
3: [![GitHub
4: release](https://img.shields.io/github/release/tmux-plugins/tmux-yank.svg)](https://github.com/tmux-plugins/tmux-yank/releases)
5: [![GitHub
6: issues](https://img.shields.io/github/issues/tmux-plugins/tmux-yank.svg)](https://github.com/tmux-plugins/tmux-yank/issues)
7: 
8: tmux-yank
9: =========
10: 
11: Copy to the system clipboard in [`tmux`](https://tmux.github.io/).
12: 
13: Supports:
14: 
15: -   Linux
16: -   macOS
17: -   Cygwin
18: -   Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)
19: 
20: Installing
21: ----------
22: 
23: ### Via TPM (recommended)
24: 
25: The easiest way to install `tmux-yank` is via the [Tmux Plugin
26: Manager](https://github.com/tmux-plugins/tpm).
27: 
28: 1.  Add plugin to the list of TPM plugins in `.tmux.conf`:
29: 
30:     ``` tmux
31:     set -g @plugin 'tmux-plugins/tmux-yank'
32:     ```
33: 
34: 2.  Use <kbd>prefix</kbd>–<kbd>I</kbd> install `tmux-yank`. You should now
35:     be able to `tmux-yank` immediately.
36: 3.  When you want to update `tmux-yank` use <kbd>prefix</kbd>–<kbd>U</kbd>.
37: 
38: ### Manual Installation
39: 
40: 1.  Clone the repository
41: 
42:     ``` sh
43:     $ git clone https://github.com/tmux-plugins/tmux-yank ~/clone/path
44:     ```
45: 
46: 2.  Add this line to the bottom of `.tmux.conf`
47: 
48:     ``` tmux
49:     run-shell ~/clone/path/yank.tmux
50:     ```
51: 
52: 3.  Reload the `tmux` environment
53: 
54:     ``` sh
55:     # type this inside tmux
56:     $ tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf
57:     ```
58: 
59: You should now be able to use `tmux-yank` immediately.
60: 
61: Requirements
62: ------------
63: 
64: In order for `tmux-yank` to work, there must be a program that store data in
65: the system clipboard.
66: 
67: ### macOS
68: 
69: -   [`reattach-to-user-namespace`](https://github.com/ChrisJohnsen/tmux-MacOSX-pasteboard)
70: 
71: **Note**: Some versions of macOS (aka OS X) have been reported to work
72: without `reattach-to-user-namespace`. It doesn't hurt to have it installed.
73: 
74: -   OS X 10.8: Mountain Lion – *required*
75: -   OS X 10.9: Mavericks – *required*
76: -   OS X 10.10: Yosemite – *not required*
77: -   OS X 10.11: El Capitan – *not required*
78: -   macOS 10.12: Sierra – *required*
79: -   macOS 10.14: Mojave - *required*
80: -   macOS 10.15: Catalina - *not required*
81: 
82: The easiest way to use `reattach-to-user-namespace` with `tmux` is use to
83: use the [`tmux-sensible`](https://github.com/tmux-plugins/tmux-sensible)
84: plugin.
85: 
86: To use it manually, use:
87: 
88: ``` tmux
89: # ~/.tmux.conf
90: set-option -g default-command "reattach-to-user-namespace -l $SHELL"
91: ```
92: 
93: If you have `tmux` 1.5 or newer and are using
94: [iTerm2](https://www.iterm2.com/) version 3 or newer then the <kbd>y</kbd>
95: in `copy-mode` and mouse selection will work without `tmux-yank`.
96: 
97: To enable this:
98: 
99: 1.  Go into iTerm2's preferences.
100: 2.  Go to the "General" tab.
101: 3.  Check "Applications in terminal may access clipboard"
102: 4.  In `tmux`, ensure `set-clipboard` is turned on:
103: 
104:     ``` sh
105:     $ tmux show-options -g -s set-clipboard
106:     set-clipboard on
107:     ```
108: 
109: #### [HomeBrew](https://brew.sh/) (recommended)
110: 
111: ``` sh
112: $ brew install reattach-to-user-namespace
113: ```
114: 
115: #### MacPorts
116: 
117: ``` sh
118: $ sudo port install tmux-pasteboard
119: ```
120: 
121: ### Linux
122: 
123: -   `xsel` (recommended) or `xclip` (for X).
124: -   `wl-copy` from [wl-clipboard](https://github.com/bugaevc/wl-clipboard) (for Wayland)
125: 
126: If you have `tmux` 1.5 or newer and are using `xterm`, the <kbd>y</kbd> in
127: `copy-mode` and mouse selection will work without `tmux-yank`. See the
128: `tmux(1)` man page entry for the `set-clipboard` option.
129: 
130: #### Debian & Ubuntu
131: 
132: ``` sh
133: $ sudo apt-get install xsel # or xclip
134: ```
135: 
136: #### RedHat & CentOS
137: 
138: ``` sh
139: $ sudo yum install xsel # or xclip
140: ```
141: 
142: ### Cygwin
143: 
144: -   (*optional*) `putclip` which is part of the `cygutils-extra` package.
145: 
146: ### Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)
147: 
148: -   `clip.exe` is shipped with Windows Subsystem for Linux.
149: 
150: Configuration
151: -------------
152: 
153: ### Key bindings
154: 
155: -   Normal Mode
156:     -   <kbd>prefix</kbd>–<kbd>y</kbd> — copies text from the command line
157:         to the clipboard.
158: 
159:         Works with all popular shells/repls. Tested with:
160: 
161:         -   shells: `bash`, `zsh` (with `bindkey -e`), `tcsh`
162:         -   repls: `irb`, `pry`, `node`, `psql`, `python`, `php -a`,
163:             `coffee`
164:         -   remote shells: `ssh`, [mosh](http://mosh.mit.edu/)
165:         -   vim/neovim command line (requires
166:             [vim-husk](https://github.com/bruno-/vim-husk) or
167:             [vim-rsi](https://github.com/tpope/vim-rsi) plugin)
168: 
169:     -   <kbd>prefix</kbd>–<kbd>Y</kbd> — copy the current pane's current
170:         working directory to the clipboard.
171: 
172: -   Copy Mode
173:     -   <kbd>y</kbd> — copy selection to system clipboard.
174:     -   <kbd>Y</kbd> (shift-y) — "put" selection. Equivalent to copying a
175:         selection, and pasting it to the command line.
176: 
177: 
178: ### Default and Preferred Clipboard Programs
179: 
180: tmux-yank does its best to detect a reasonable choice for a clipboard
181: program on your OS.
182: 
183: If tmux-yank can't detect a known clipboard program then it uses the
184: `@custom_copy_command` tmux option as your clipboard program if set.
185: 
186: If you need to always override tmux-yank's choice for a clipboard program,
187: then you can set `@override_copy_command` to force tmux-yank to use whatever
188: you want.
189: 
190: Note that both programs _must_ accept `STDIN` for the text to be copied.
191: 
192: An example of setting `@override_copy_command`:
193: 
194: ``` tmux
195: # ~/.tmux.conf
196: 
197: set -g @custom_copy_command 'my-clipboard-copy --some-arg'
198: # or
199: set -g @override_copy_command 'my-clipboard-copy --some-arg'
200: ```
201: 
202: ### Linux Clipboards
203: 
204: Linux has several cut-and-paste clipboards: `primary`, `secondary`, and
205: `clipboard` (default in tmux-yank is `clipboard`).
206: 
207: You can change this by setting `@yank_selection`:
208: 
209: ``` tmux
210: # ~/.tmux.conf
211: 
212: set -g @yank_selection 'primary' # or 'secondary' or 'clipboard'
213: ```
214: 
215: With mouse support turned on (see below) the default clipboard for mouse
216: selections is `primary`.
217: 
218: You can change this by setting `@yank_selection_mouse`:
219: 
220: ``` tmux
221: # ~/.tmux.conf
222: 
223: set -g @yank_selection_mouse 'clipboard' # or 'primary' or 'secondary'
224: ```
225: 
226: ### Controlling Yank Behavior
227: 
228: By default, `tmux-yank` will exit copy mode after yanking text. If you wish to
229: remain in copy mode, you can set `@yank_action`:
230: 
231: ``` tmux
232: # ~/.tmux.conf
233: 
234: set -g @yank_action 'copy-pipe' # or 'copy-pipe-and-cancel' for the default
235: ```
236: 
237: ### Mouse Support
238: 
239: `tmux-yank` has mouse support enabled by default. It will only work if `tmux`'s
240: built-in mouse support is also enabled (with `mouse on` since `tmux` 2.1, or
241: `mode-mouse on` in older versions).
242: 
243: To yank with the mouse, click and drag with the primary button to begin
244: selection, and release to yank.
245: 
246: If you would prefer to disable this behavior, or provide your own bindings for
247: the `MouseDragEnd1Pane` event, you can do so with:
248: 
249: ``` tmux
250: # ~/.tmux.conf
251: 
252: set -g @yank_with_mouse off # or 'on'
253: ```
254: 
255: If you want to remain in copy mode after making a mouse selection, set
256: `@yank_action` as described above.
257: 
258: ### vi mode support

</content>

**Considering tool outputs**

I’m pondering if tool outputs are environment-managed. It seems like the system preserves task, skill, and other elements during compression, but doesn’t keep the content of those outputs. I need to consider how to include key information, despite compression possibly removing the user’s prompt. Should I wait to do this extraction until after finalizing everything? There’s a lot to keep straight here, and I want to get it right!

content.summary:
User reported: "We deleted the transparent theme but now Opencode isn't fully transparent... also yank in copy mode isn't working". Work was in read/plan-oriented investigation only, no files edited.

Key repo/context constraints: dotfiles repo at `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles`; opencode config customization skill was loaded because this is OpenCode config work. Skill says OpenCode strict-validates config, global config lives at `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json`, TUI config/theme changes require quitting/restarting OpenCode. Dotfiles repo instructions say tmux config is only root `.tmux.conf`; do not create `.config/tmux/tmux.conf`; use GNU Stow; Fish primary shell.

OpenCode files found and inspected:
- `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.config/opencode/opencode.json`: valid JSON, `$schema` `https://opencode.ai/config.json`; providers include gitlab, anthropic via local baseURL, openai with `gpt-5.5` and `gpt-5.4`; default `model` is `openai/gpt-5.5`; `small_model` is `anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5`; plugins include `@tarquinen/opencode-dcp@latest`, `opencode-pty`, `opencode-vibeguard`, `./plugin/nvim-open.ts`; `permission` is blanket `

parts.text: ▣ DCP | -73.2K removed, +10.8K summary — Compression #1

**Analyzing tmux configuration**

I see that I have tmux settings showing `set-clipboard`, `allow-passthrough`, and the presence of `pbcopy`. However, there’s an issue because yank is not working. I think I need to read relevant lines in the `.tmux.conf` and use `grep` for terms like `copy-mode`, `reattach`, and `pbcopy`. I should also inspect the terminal configuration for transparency, particularly for the user's OpenCode setup, and look into the themes since an old transparent theme seems to have been deleted.

**Deciding on theme restoration**

I need to restore the custom transparent theme or find a complete theme to override any built-in transparency issues. I should inspect the OpenCode theme schema—maybe I can fetch it from the OpenCode site, or check if I have it cached. Knowing the color keys will help since the old theme is likely a match. But I wonder if that will resolve the issue, especially since not all elements may be transparent even with the old theme.

I’ll stay read-only and verify the current OpenCode/Tmux config surfaces before proposing the implementation plan. The likely shape is restoring a custom `transparent` theme with every background-capable key set to `none`, then simplifying tmux copy bindings around native `pbcopy`.

parts.text:
**Analyzing current theme file**

I'm looking into the current `tui.json` file at line 3 for the theme system, which seems interesting. It looks like the older version from Git might be compressed, but I'm not sure if it's the current version or just an older one that had transparency. I definitely need to read through it more carefully to figure that out. Let's see what I can find!

Two current-state differences are important: `tui.json` now uses `theme: "system"` and there is no `themes/transparent.json` in the repo, so OpenCode is falling back to its built-in/current system theme rather than the deleted transparent override. The tmux source config already avoids `reattach-to-user-namespace`, but the live bindings from the earlier check still showed stale `reattach` bindings, which points to tmux not being reloaded or an overriding plugin/session state.

# Themes

Select a built-in theme or define your own.

With OpenCode you can select from one of several built-in themes, use a theme that adapts to your terminal theme, or define your own custom theme.

By default, OpenCode uses our own `opencode` theme.

---

## [Terminal requirements](#terminal-requirements)

For themes to display correctly with their full color palette, your terminal must support **truecolor** (24-bit color). Most modern terminals support this by default, but you may need to enable it:

-   **Check support**: Run `echo $COLORTERM` - it should output `truecolor` or `24bit`
-   **Enable truecolor**: Set the environment variable `COLORTERM=truecolor` in your shell profile
-   **Terminal compatibility**: Ensure your terminal emulator supports 24-bit color (most modern terminals like iTerm2, Alacritty, Kitty, Windows Terminal, and recent versions of GNOME Terminal do)

Without truecolor support, themes may appear with reduced color accuracy or fall back to the nearest 256-color approximation.

---

## [Built-in themes](#built-in-themes)

OpenCode comes with several built-in themes.

Name

Description

`system`

Adapts to your terminal’s background color

`tokyonight`

Based on the [Tokyonight](https://github.com/folke/tokyonight.nvim) theme

`everforest`

Based on the [Everforest](https://github.com/sainnhe/everforest) theme

`ayu`

Based on the [Ayu](https://github.com/ayu-theme) dark theme

`catppuccin`

Based on the [Catppuccin](https://github.com/catppuccin) theme

`catppuccin-macchiato`

Based on the [Catppuccin](https://github.com/catppuccin) theme

`gruvbox`

Based on the [Gruvbox](https://github.com/morhetz/gruvbox) theme

`kanagawa`

Based on the [Kanagawa](https://github.com/rebelot/kanagawa.nvim) theme

`nord`

Based on the [Nord](https://github.com/nordtheme/nord) theme

`matrix`

Hacker-style green on black theme

`one-dark`

Based on the [Atom One](https://github.com/Th3Whit3Wolf/one-nvim) Dark theme

And more, we are constantly adding new themes.

---

## [System theme](#system-theme)

The `system` theme is designed to automatically adapt to your terminal’s color scheme. Unlike traditional themes that use fixed colors, the *system* theme:

-   **Generates gray scale**: Creates a custom gray scale based on your terminal’s background color, ensuring optimal contrast.
-   **Uses ANSI colors**: Leverages standard ANSI colors (0-15) for syntax highlighting and UI elements, which respect your terminal’s color palette.
-   **Preserves terminal defaults**: Uses `none` for text and background colors to maintain your terminal’s native appearance.

The system theme is for users who:

-   Want OpenCode to match their terminal’s appearance
-   Use custom terminal color schemes
-   Prefer a consistent look across all terminal applications

---

## [Using a theme](#using-a-theme)

You can select a theme by bringing up the theme select with the `/theme` command. Or you can specify it in `tui.json`.

tui.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/tui.json",  "theme": "tokyonight"}
```

---

## [Custom themes](#custom-themes)

OpenCode supports a flexible JSON-based theme system that allows users to create and customize themes easily.

---

### [Hierarchy](#hierarchy)

Themes are loaded from multiple directories in the following order where later directories override earlier ones:

1.  **Built-in themes** - These are embedded in the binary
2.  **User config directory** - Defined in `~/.config/opencode/themes/*.json` or `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/opencode/themes/*.json`
3.  **Project root directory** - Defined in the `<project-root>/.opencode/themes/*.json`
4.  **Current working directory** - Defined in `./.opencode/themes/*.json`

If multiple directories contain a theme with the same name, the theme from the directory with higher priority will be used.

---

### [Creating a theme](#creating-a-theme)

To create a custom theme, create a JSON file in one of the theme directories.

For user-wide themes:

Terminal window

```
mkdir -p ~/.config/opencode/themesvim ~/.config/opencode/themes/my-theme.json
```

And for project-specific themes.

Terminal window

```
mkdir -p .opencode/themesvim .opencode/themes/my-theme.json
```

---

### [JSON format](#json-format)

Themes use a flexible JSON format with support for:

-   **Hex colors**: `"#ffffff"`
-   **ANSI colors**: `3` (0-255)
-   **Color references**: `"primary"` or custom definitions
-   **Dark/light variants**: `{"dark": "#000", "light": "#fff"}`
-   **No color**: `"none"` - Uses the terminal’s default color or transparent

---

### [Color definitions](#color-definitions)

The `defs` section is optional and it allows you to define reusable colors that can be referenced in the theme.

---

### [Terminal defaults](#terminal-defaults)

The special value `"none"` can be used for any color to inherit the terminal’s default color. This is particularly useful for creating themes that blend seamlessly with your terminal’s color scheme:

-   `"text": "none"` - Uses terminal’s default foreground color
-   `"background": "none"` - Uses terminal’s default background color

---

### [Example](#example)

Here’s an example of a custom theme:

my-theme.json

```
